draft.introduction: The Hypostasis of the Archons (“The Reality of the Rulers”) is a Gnostic rewriting of Genesis found in the Nag Hammadi library (Codex II, Tractate 4). Composed in Coptic, likely translated from a Greek original of the second or third century, it opens by quoting Paul—“Our struggle is not against flesh and blood, but against the rulers of this world” (Ephesians 6:12)—and then proceeds to explain exactly who those rulers are. The text retells the creation of the world, the making of Adam, the role of Eve, and the expulsion from Eden, but inverts the meaning of each event. The archons (rulers) who create the material world are not the true God but blind, arrogant powers. The serpent is not the enemy but the instructor. Eve is not the cause of the fall but the agent of awakening. The true God is hidden, and the material creation is a prison. The text is essential for understanding how Gnostic communities read Genesis as a story of divine sparks trapped in matter, and how they understood salvation as the soul’s recognition of its true origin.

translation.text: On account of the reality of the authorities, inspired by the spirit of the father of truth, the great apostle—referring to the “authorities of the darkness”—told us that “our contest is not against flesh and blood; rather, the authorities of the universe and the spirits of wickedness.”

Their chief is blind. Because of his power and his ignorance and his arrogance he said, with his power, “It is I who am God; there is none apart from me.” When he said this, he sinned against the entirety. And this speech reached up to incorruptibility; and there was a voice that came forth from incorruptibility, saying, “You are mistaken, Samael”—which means, “blind god.”

The rulers laid plans and said, “Come, let us create a human being out of dust from the earth.” They modelled their creature as one entirely of the earth. The rulers took dust from the earth and modelled their human being, after the body of the rulers and after the image of God that had appeared to them in the waters.

They said to one another, “Come, let us cause a deep sleep to fall upon Adam.” And he slept. Now the deep sleep that they caused to fall upon him was ignorance. They opened his side, which was like a living woman. And they built up the side with flesh in her place, and Adam came to be entirely of soul only.
translation.original_text: ⲓⲉⲵⲁⲥ ⲙⲓⲓⲉⲥⲉⲥⲣⲉⲥ ⲵⲉ ⲣⲁⲣⲕⲓⲓ ⲉⲧⲕⲥⲕⲉⲓ ⲉⲧⲃⲉ ⲁⲇⲁⲙ

translation.text: The spirit-endowed woman came to him and spoke with him, saying, “Arise, Adam.” And when he saw her, he said, “It is you who have given me life; you shall be called ‘mother of the living.’ For it is she who is my mother. It is she who is the physician, and the woman, and she who has given birth.”
translation.original_text: ⲧⲓⲓⲓⲙⲁⲧⲓⲕⲕⲣ ⲇⲉ ⲁⲩⲉⲓ ⲉⲧⲕⲥⲧⲩ ⲁⲇⲁⲙ ⲵⲉ ⲓⲉⲵⲁⲥ

translation.text: Then the authorities came to their Adam. And when they saw his female counterpart speaking with him, they became agitated and lusted after her. They said to one another, “Come, let us sow our seed in her.” But the spiritual woman laughed at them, for they were blind and could not see her. She became a tree, and left before them her shadowy reflection resembling herself.
translation.original_text: ⲁⲣⲑⲕⲣ ⲇⲉ ⲁⲥⲉⲓ ⲉⲣⲉ ⲣⲁⲣⲕⲓⲓ ⲉⲧⲃⲉ ⲓⲉⲥⲁⲇⲁⲙ

translation.text: Then the female spiritual principle came in the form of the snake, the instructor. And it taught them, saying, “You shall not die a death of dying. For it was out of jealousy that he said this to you. Rather, your eyes shall open, and you shall become like gods, recognizing evil and good.”

translator_notes.note: Samael (“blind god”) is the Gnostic name for the Demiurge, the creator of the material world. His declaration “I am God; there is none apart from me” quotes Isaiah 45:5 and 46:9. In the orthodox reading, these are statements of monotheistic truth. In the Gnostic reading, they are the arrogant boasts of a lesser, ignorant being who does not know the true God above him.

The identification of Adam’s “deep sleep” (Genesis 2:21) with ignorance is characteristic of Gnostic exegesis. In this reading, the creation of Eve is not a gift but a trap: the archons are trying to separate the spiritual element from Adam. However, the text also subverts this by making the “living woman” the spiritual principle herself.

The serpent as “instructor” is the most famous Gnostic inversion of Genesis. The knowledge of good and evil is not a curse but the beginning of liberation. The serpent represents the true divine wisdom breaking into the prison of the archons’ creation. This is why the Church Fathers sometimes called the Gnostics “Ophites” (from ophis, “serpent”).
